menstrual.title: Menstrual Phase
menstrual.what_happens: This is the phase most people are familiar with — it is when bleeding occurs. The uterine lining that built up during the previous cycle sheds because pregnancy did not take place. For most people, this phase lasts between 3 and 7 days. Hormone levels, particularly estrogen and progesterone, are at their lowest during this time.
menstrual.how_pcos_affects_this: In PCOS, periods may be infrequent, very light, unusually heavy, or sometimes absent for months at a time. This happens because the hormonal signals that trigger the shedding of the uterine lining are often irregular. When ovulation does not occur in the previous cycle, the lining can build up for longer than usual, leading to heavier or more unpredictable bleeding when a period does eventually arrive. Some women with PCOS go through long stretches without a period, which is medically referred to as oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea.
menstrual.common_experiences: Women with PCOS may notice that their periods do not follow a predictable pattern from month to month. Some experience very heavy flow after a long gap, while others may have very light spotting. Cramping can vary widely. The unpredictability itself is often one of the most frustrating aspects of this phase for those living with PCOS.
menstrual.why_it_matters: Understanding that irregular periods in PCOS are a hormonal pattern rather than a random occurrence can reduce some of the anxiety that comes with unpredictability. Tracking what your periods are like, including how long they last and how heavy the flow is, gives you useful information to share with your doctor and helps you understand your own body's unique pattern over time.

follicular.title: Follicular Phase
follicular.what_happens: The follicular phase begins on the first day of your period and continues until ovulation. During this time, the brain sends a signal in the form of follicle-stimulating hormone, or FSH, which encourages small fluid-filled sacs called follicles in the ovaries to grow. Each follicle contains an egg. As these follicles develop, they produce estrogen, which gradually rises and helps the uterine lining begin rebuilding. Typically, one follicle becomes dominant and prepares to release its egg.
follicular.how_pcos_affects_this: In PCOS, the follicular phase is where much of the disruption originates. The ovaries often develop many small follicles, which is what gives the condition its name, but these follicles frequently do not mature fully or compete against each other without a clear dominant follicle emerging. FSH levels may be insufficient or the follicles may be resistant to its signal. Elevated levels of luteinizing hormone, or LH, which are common in PCOS, can also interfere with normal follicle development. As a result, this phase may stretch on for weeks or even months rather than the typical 10 to 14 days.
follicular.common_experiences: Because this phase can extend significantly in PCOS, women may experience long stretches of what feels like a waiting period between cycles. Energy levels and mood, which tend to improve as estrogen rises during a typical follicular phase, may feel less predictable. Some women notice mild pelvic discomfort as multiple follicles develop simultaneously in the ovaries.
follicular.why_it_matters: Knowing that the follicular phase is where cycle irregularity often begins in PCOS helps make sense of why cycles can be so long and unpredictable. It is not that the body has forgotten what to do — it is that the hormonal coordination required to move through this phase smoothly is being disrupted. This understanding can make the experience feel less alarming and more manageable.

ovulation.title: Ovulation Phase
ovulation.what_happens: Ovulation is the moment when a mature egg is released from the dominant follicle in the ovary. This is triggered by a sharp surge in luteinizing hormone, or LH. The released egg travels down the fallopian tube and is available to be fertilised for approximately 12 to 24 hours. Estrogen peaks just before ovulation, and after the egg is released, progesterone begins to rise. In a typical cycle, ovulation happens around day 14, though this varies from person to person.
ovulation.how_pcos_affects_this: Ovulation is one of the most significantly affected phases in PCOS. Because follicles often fail to mature fully, the LH surge needed to trigger ovulation may not occur properly or at the expected time. This means ovulation may be delayed, occur unpredictably, or in many cases not happen at all during a given cycle. When a cycle passes without ovulation, it is called an anovulatory cycle. Women with PCOS may have some cycles where ovulation does occur and others where it does not, making it genuinely difficult to know when or if it is happening.
ovulation.common_experiences: Because ovulation in PCOS is irregular, the usual signs that some women notice, such as a slight twinge in the lower abdomen, changes in cervical fluid, or a very brief dip and rise in basal body temperature, may be absent, unclear, or occur at unexpected times. Some women with PCOS experience what feels like prolonged pre-ovulatory symptoms because the body makes repeated attempts to ovulate before eventually succeeding or moving on without ovulating.
ovulation.why_it_matters: Understanding that ovulation is the central event that links all other phases of the cycle helps explain why its disruption in PCOS creates a ripple effect across the entire cycle. An irregular or absent ovulation directly affects what happens in the luteal phase, when the next period arrives, and overall hormonal balance. It is also the key factor in fertility, which is why many women with PCOS who are trying to conceive work with a doctor specifically around this phase.

luteal.title: Luteal Phase
luteal.what_happens: After ovulation, the empty follicle transforms into a temporary structure called the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone is the dominant hormone of this phase and its job is to prepare the uterine lining for a potential pregnancy. If fertilisation does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down after approximately 10 to 14 days, progesterone drops, and the cycle ends with the next period beginning. This phase is generally more consistent in length than the follicular phase.
luteal.how_pcos_affects_this: In PCOS, the luteal phase is affected by what happens before it. If ovulation did not occur, a corpus luteum does not form and progesterone levels remain low or absent. This creates a state where estrogen goes relatively unopposed by progesterone, which can contribute to the uterine lining continuing to build up. Low progesterone is associated with irregular cycles, missed periods, and is also linked to the mood and emotional changes that many women with PCOS experience. When the luteal phase does occur after ovulation, it can sometimes be shorter than typical, which is referred to as a luteal phase defect.
luteal.common_experiences: Women with PCOS who do experience a luteal phase may notice heightened PMS symptoms, including mood changes, bloating, breast tenderness, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. These symptoms can sometimes feel more intense than in women without PCOS, partly due to the relative imbalance between estrogen and progesterone. For those whose cycles are very long or anovulatory, there may be a prolonged phase of uncertainty about when or whether a period will arrive, which carries its own emotional weight.
luteal.why_it_matters: The luteal phase is often overlooked in conversations about PCOS because it comes at the end of the cycle, but understanding it helps explain both the physical symptoms that precede a period and the emotional shifts that can occur. It also clarifies why hormonal imbalance in PCOS is not just about irregular periods but about a pattern of disruption that plays out across the entire cycle. Recognising this full picture helps women advocate for themselves more clearly when speaking with healthcare providers.
